content.text: Adding a service to the catalog is a one-time act; keeping the catalog accurate over the full service lifetime requires lifecycle rules. Without them, the catalog fills with abandoned prototypes, deprecated APIs with no consumers, and ghost services whose infrastructure was deleted months ago. This sub-subtopic defines the onboarding gate, the four lifecycle states, the rules that govern transitions between them, and the automated checks that keep the catalog clean.
field. The platform enforces a controlled vocabulary of four values; free-form values fail the catalog linter.
Spike, proof-of-concept, or active development. Not production-ready.
Internal to the owning team only
Live, supported, and scored by the nightly scorecard.
Any team with the required scope
Still running but scheduled for removal. No new consumers.
Scorecard still runs; badge shows orange
Existing consumers only; new integrations blocked
Decommissioned. Infrastructure removed. Catalog entry archived for audit.
Services that predate the catalog program are onboarded with
directly if they are already in production, skipping the experimental phase. They are given a 30-day grace period to reach the scorecard gate before violations are enforced.
A service can only move from
after passing all gate checks. The gate is enforced by the catalog promotion workflow — a GitHub Actions workflow that the team triggers manually when they believe the service is ready.
Owner is a Backstage Group (not an individual user)
Catalog API query: spec.owner must reference a Group entity
Nightly scorecard must have run at least once and scored ≥ 80%
TechDocs build CI step must have passed; /docs/product-search-rag/ must return 200
HTTP GET on genai.io/eval-suite-url must return 200
genai.io/data-class=restricted requires a signed approval ticket URL in the annotation
catalog-info.yaml links section must include a link with icon: article

# .github/workflows/catalog-promote.yml
# Triggered by: gh workflow run catalog-promote.yml
name: Promote to Production
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      catalog_path:
        description: Path to catalog-info.yaml
        required: true
        default: catalog-info.yaml

jobs:
  gate-check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Install tools
        run: pip install pyyaml httpx

      - name: Lint catalog-info
        run: python scripts/lint-catalog-info.py ${{ inputs.catalog_path }}

      - name: Check TechDocs
        run: |
          NAME=$(python -c "import yaml,sys; d=yaml.safe_load(open(sys.argv[1])); print(d['metadata']['name'])" ${{ inputs.catalog_path }})
          curl -sf "https://backstage.internal/docs/$NAME/" || (echo "TechDocs not found"; exit 1)

      - name: Verify eval suite URL
        run: |
          URL=$(python -c "import yaml,sys; d=yaml.safe_load(open(sys.argv[1])); print(d['metadata']['annotations'].get('genai.io/eval-suite-url',''))" ${{ inputs.catalog_path }})
          [ -z "$URL" ] && echo "WARNING: No eval suite URL" || curl -sf "$URL" || (echo "Eval suite URL unreachable"; exit 1)

      - name: Set lifecycle to production
        run: |
          python - <<'PY'
          import yaml, pathlib, sys
          path = "${{ inputs.catalog_path }}"
          doc  = yaml.safe_load(pathlib.Path(path).read_text())
          doc["spec"]["lifecycle"] = "production"
          pathlib.Path(path).write_text(yaml.dump(doc, default_flow_style=False))
          PY

      - uses: stefanzweifel/git-auto-commit-action@v5
        with:
          commit_message: "catalog: promote ${{ inputs.catalog_path }} to production"
When a team decides to retire a service, they open a deprecation PR that sets
and adds the required deprecation annotations. The PR description must include a migration guide URL and target EOL date.

# Additions to catalog-info.yaml when deprecating
metadata:
  annotations:
    # ... existing annotations ...
    genai.io/deprecation-date: "2026-07-01"
    genai.io/eol-date:         "2026-10-01"      # 90-day window after deprecation
    genai.io/migration-guide:  https://wiki.internal/migrate-to-v2-search
    genai.io/replacement:      product-search-rag-v2   # Backstage component name
spec:
  lifecycle: deprecated
Once the lifecycle is set to
, the platform automation applies the following changes within one hour:
Backstage catalog badge changes to orange; the component page shows a deprecation banner with the EOL date and migration guide link.
The API gateway injects a Deprecation response header on every request: Deprecation: Sat, 01 Oct 2026 00:00:00 GMT.
A Slack message is sent to every team that has registered the component in their spec.dependsOn, listing the EOL date and migration guide.
New catalog registrations that reference the deprecated component in spec.dependsOn are blocked by the catalog linter.
On the EOL date, the platform team runs the decommission workflow. The team must confirm that all consumers have migrated before the workflow proceeds.
After EOL, the infrastructure decommission checklist must be completed:
Scale down and delete the Kubernetes deployment and HPA.
Delete the LLM gateway route entry for this service.
Revoke the service's API credentials in Vault.
Archive the vector store index (snapshot to cold storage; do not delete until the data retention period expires).
Set spec.lifecycle to end-of-life in catalog-info.yaml and open a PR to the platform-catalog repo.
Retain the catalog entry in read-only archived state for 12 months for audit trail purposes.
Components that have not been updated for 90 days and have a
are flagged as stale by the nightly pipeline. The owning team receives a Slack prompt asking whether the component is still active, deprecated, or should be promoted to production.

from datetime import datetime, timezone
import httpx

def find_stale_experimental(threshold_days: int = 90) -> list[dict]:
    resp = httpx.get(
        "https://backstage.internal/api/catalog/entities",
        params={"kind": "Component", "filter": "spec.lifecycle=experimental"},
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {TOKEN}"},
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    stale = []
    for entity in resp.json():
        updated_raw = (entity.get("metadata", {})
                             .get("annotations", {})
                             .get("backstage.io/managed-by-location", ""))
        # Use the catalog's updateAt timestamp (from the entity envelope)
        updated_at = entity.get("metadata", {}).get("updatedAt")
        if updated_at:
            age_days = (now - datetime.fromisoformat(updated_at)).days
            if age_days >= threshold_days:
                stale.append({
                    "name":     entity["metadata"]["name"],
                    "owner":    entity["spec"].get("owner"),
                    "age_days": age_days,
                })
    return stale

— registered URL patterns that the catalog's entity provider scans on a configurable schedule (default: every 3 minutes). The platform team pre-registers a GitHub organisation-wide location so that any repo with a
at its root is automatically discovered and ingested without manual registration.

# app-config.yaml (Backstage instance config, managed by platform team)
catalog:
  providers:
    github:
      production:
        organization: acme-corp
        catalogPath: /catalog-info.yaml    # root of every repo
        filters:
          branch: main
          repository: '.*'                 # all repos
        schedule:
          frequency: { minutes: 3 }
          timeout:   { minutes: 3 }
The implication: a team registers their service in the catalog simply by merging a valid
. Within 3 minutes the entity appears in the Backstage UI. There is no manual registration step. Conversely, deleting the file from
removes the entity from the catalog within the same window — which is why the EOL workflow archives the entry rather than deleting the file.

The catalog contains fields that affect platform-level controls: data classification, cost attribution, and model tier. Changes to these fields must go through a review process to prevent teams from downgrading their classification to escape controls.
Team tech lead + security team approval
Team tech lead + platform team approval
DPO must be notified within 24h of change
These restrictions are enforced by a CODEOWNERS rule on the
file requires approval from the relevant team when sensitive annotations change:

# .github/CODEOWNERS
# catalog-info.yaml changes require platform team + security review
catalog-info.yaml   @acme-corp/platform-ai @acme-corp/security-team
The nightly catalog audit script compares annotation values in
with the runtime signals emitted by the service (e.g., checking that a service annotated as
in its Prometheus metrics). Drift between declared and observed values triggers a scorecard violation.
Consumer Impact Analysis Before Lifecycle Transitions

Before transitioning a component to deprecated or end-of-life, the owning team must run a consumer impact analysis. The platform provides a script that queries the catalog graph and the API gateway logs to produce a complete picture of active consumers.

(teams making API calls in the gateway logs but not registered in the catalog). Both must migrate before EOL proceeds. Undeclared traffic consumers are the most common reason for blocked decommissions.

Service catalog lifecycle rules prevent entropy. The four states — experimental, production, deprecated, end-of-life — give every component a clear status that consumers can rely on. The production onboarding gate ensures services are fully observable, documented, and evaluated before they receive external consumers. Deprecation rules protect existing consumers with a 90-day migration window and automated header injection. The decommission workflow prevents accidental removal of components that still have active dependants. Stale experimental detection keeps the catalog free of abandoned prototypes. Together, these rules make the catalog a trustworthy source of truth rather than a snapshot that decays over time.
